title: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Critical Care Fellows
objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation and underlying causes of DIC
Apply the ISTH DIC scoring system for diagnosis
Understand the pathophysiology of simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding
Interpret coagulation studies in DIC (PT, PTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer, platelets)
Implement treatment prioritizing the underlying cause
Distinguish DIC phenotypes: bleeding vs organ failure/thrombotic

guidelineReferences.name: ISTH DIC Scoring System
guidelineReferences.citation: Taylor FB, et al. Towards definition, clinical and laboratory criteria, and a scoring system for disseminated intravascular coagulation. Thromb Haemost. 2001;86:1327-1330
keyPoints: Components: platelet count, D-dimer/FDP, PT prolongation, fibrinogen
Requires underlying disorder associated with DIC

guidelineReferences.name: BCSH Guidelines for DIC
guidelineReferences.citation: Levi M, et al. Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of disseminated intravascular coagulation. Br J Haematol. 2009;145:24-33

guidelineReferences.name: ISTH Harmonized Guidance
guidelineReferences.citation: Wada H, et al. Guidance for diagnosis and treatment of DIC from harmonization of the recommendations from three guidelines. J Thromb Haemost. 2013

vignette.text:
A 58-year-old man is transferred from the medical floor to the ICU with septic shock from pneumonia. He was admitted 2 days ago with community-acquired pneumonia and has deteriorated despite antibiotics. He now has hypotension requiring vasopressors, acute kidney injury, and new bleeding from IV sites and his gums.

Past Medical History: Type 2 diabetes, Hypertension, COPD

Medications: Metformin (held), Lisinopril (held), Tiotropium

Allergies: None

Hospital Course: Started on ceftriaxone and azithromycin. Blood cultures growing Streptococcus pneumoniae. Developed hypotension on day 2, now on norepinephrine.

physicalExam.skin: Petechiae on trunk, ecchymoses at IV sites, mottled extremities
physicalExam.pulmonary: Coarse breath sounds bilaterally, ventilator settings: AC 14, TV 450, PEEP 8

imaging.chestXray: Bilateral infiltrates consistent with pneumonia, no obvious pulmonary embolism

questions.stem: This septic patient has thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, and bleeding.
questions.question: What is DIC and what are the major underlying causes?
questions.hint: Consider the MOST COMMON triggers of DIC.

options.text: Acquired coagulopathy with systemic activation of coagulation leading to microthrombosis AND consumption of clotting factors/platelets causing bleeding; Major causes: Sepsis (most common), Trauma, Malignancy, Obstetric complications (abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, HELLP)
Only occurs with liver disease
Inherited bleeding disorder
Vitamin K deficiency only

questions.explanation: DIC is an ACQUIRED syndrome of systemic coagulation activation causing: (1) Microvascular thrombosis → organ dysfunction; (2) Consumption of platelets and clotting factors → bleeding. Major causes: SEPSIS (30-50% of cases, most common), Trauma/burns, Malignancy (solid tumors, acute promyelocytic leukemia), Obstetric (placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, HELLP, retained dead fetus), Vascular abnormalities (large aneurysms), Severe transfusion reactions, Envenomation. This patient has sepsis-induced DIC.

whyChain.answer: Sepsis triggers DIC through: (1) Tissue factor expression on monocytes/endothelium → extrinsic pathway activation; (2) Endothelial damage → loss of anticoagulant properties; (3) Impaired fibrinolysis (elevated PAI-1); (4) Cytokine storm amplifies coagulation. This creates a prothrombotic state with microvascular thrombosis causing organ failure.

Paradox of DIC: Widespread clotting CONSUMES platelets and clotting factors faster than they can be replaced. This 'consumption coagulopathy' leads to deficiency of hemostatic components → bleeding despite underlying thrombosis. Both processes occur simultaneously.

sources.text: StatPearls: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are applying the ISTH DIC scoring system to this patient.
questions.question: What are the components of the ISTH DIC scoring system?
questions.hint: Consider the four laboratory parameters and scoring thresholds.

options.text: Clinical symptoms alone diagnose DIC
Only D-dimer is needed for diagnosis
Platelet count (0-2 points), D-dimer/FDP elevation (0-3 points), Prolonged PT (0-2 points), Fibrinogen level (0-1 point); Score ≥5 = overt DIC; requires underlying disorder known to cause DIC
PTT is the main diagnostic criterion

questions.explanation: ISTH Overt DIC Score (requires underlying DIC-associated disorder): PLATELETS: ≥100K (0), 50-100K (1), <50K (2). D-DIMER/FDP: No increase (0), Moderate increase (2), Strong increase (3). PT prolongation: <3 sec (0), 3-6 sec (1), ≥6 sec (2). FIBRINOGEN: ≥100 mg/dL (0), <100 mg/dL (1). Total ≥5 = OVERT DIC. Score <5 but concerning = 'non-overt DIC' - repeat in 24 hours.

whyChain.why: What is this patient's ISTH score?
whyChain.answer: Platelets 42K = 2 points. D-dimer >20 (strong increase) = 3 points. PT 22.4 sec (prolonged ~9 sec from normal) = 2 points. Fibrinogen 95 (<100) = 1 point. TOTAL = 8 points. Score ≥5 confirms OVERT DIC. He has severe DIC.

DIC is dynamic. Serial testing (every 12-24 hours) monitors progression or improvement. A rising score indicates worsening DIC; falling score suggests response to treatment of underlying cause. Trends are often more informative than single values.

sources.text: StatPearls: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Wada H, et al. Guidance for diagnosis and treatment of DIC from harmonization of the recommendations from three guidelines. J Thromb Haemost. 2013. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are interpreting the peripheral smear showing schistocytes.
questions.question: What is the significance of schistocytes in this context?

options.text: Schistocytes indicate iron deficiency
Schistocytes only occur in hereditary conditions
Schistocytes indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) from RBC shearing across fibrin strands in microvasculature; supports DIC diagnosis but also seen in TTP/HUS - must differentiate
Schistocytes are normal findings

questions.explanation: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) - RBCs shearing across fibrin strands in microvasculature. In DIC: fibrin deposition in small vessels causes mechanical RBC fragmentation. Differential for MAHA: DIC (has underlying cause + consumptive coagulopathy), TTP (ADAMTS13 deficiency, normal coagulation), HUS (renal failure, often diarrhea-associated), HELLP, Malignant HTN, mechanical valve hemolysis. Distinguishing feature: DIC has abnormal PT/PTT/fibrinogen; TTP/HUS have NORMAL coagulation studies.

whyChain.why: How do you differentiate DIC from TTP?
whyChain.answer: Key: COAGULATION STUDIES. DIC: Prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer. TTP: NORMAL PT/PTT/fibrinogen. TTP has ADAMTS13 <10%. Both have thrombocytopenia and MAHA. This patient has abnormal coagulation → DIC, not TTP.

sources.text: StatPearls: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Wada H, et al. Guidance for diagnosis and treatment of DIC from harmonization of the recommendations from three guidelines. J Thromb Haemost. 2013. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are explaining the pathophysiology of DIC.
questions.question: What is the mechanism by which DIC causes both thrombosis and bleeding simultaneously?

options.text: Complement activation is the sole mechanism

Trigger (sepsis, trauma) → massive tissue factor release → systemic thrombin generation → widespread fibrin deposition (microthrombosis, organ failure) + consumption of platelets/factors (bleeding) + secondary fibrinolysis (elevated D-dimer)
Fibrinolysis is the only abnormality
Primary problem is platelet dysfunction only

questions.explanation: DIC pathophysiology: (1) TRIGGER (sepsis, trauma, malignancy) → tissue factor exposure/expression; (2) Tissue factor activates extrinsic pathway → THROMBIN GENERATION; (3) Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin → widespread MICROVASCULAR THROMBOSIS → organ ischemia/failure; (4) Continuous clotting CONSUMES platelets and clotting factors → BLEEDING; (5) Secondary fibrinolysis attempts to dissolve clots → ELEVATED D-DIMER (fibrin degradation products); (6) Endothelial dysfunction impairs natural anticoagulants. Result: simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding.

whyChain.why: Why is fibrinogen low but D-dimer high?
whyChain.answer: Fibrinogen is CONSUMED making fibrin clots (microthrombosis). D-dimer is PRODUCED when plasmin breaks down cross-linked fibrin. Low fibrinogen + high D-dimer = active clotting (consumption) + active clot breakdown (fibrinolysis) occurring simultaneously.

whyChain.why: What determines if patient bleeds or clots?
whyChain.answer: DIC phenotypes: (1) Bleeding-predominant: severe factor consumption, often with APL, obstetric DIC; (2) Organ failure-predominant: microthrombosis dominates, often sepsis-related, impaired fibrinolysis. This patient has organ failure (AKI, shock) + bleeding - mixed phenotype common in sepsis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Wada H, et al. Guidance for diagnosis and treatment of DIC from harmonization of the recommendations from three guidelines. J Thromb Haemost. 2013. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are developing a treatment plan for this patient's DIC.
questions.question: What is the cornerstone of DIC treatment?

options.text: Heparin for all patients
Massive transfusion regardless of bleeding
Wait for DIC to resolve spontaneously
TREAT THE UNDERLYING CAUSE - DIC will not resolve until the trigger is eliminated; for sepsis: antibiotics, source control, hemodynamic support; supportive care for coagulopathy is secondary

questions.explanation: CORNERSTONE: Treat the underlying cause. DIC is SECONDARY to another process - it will not resolve until the trigger is eliminated. For this patient with septic DIC: (1) Optimize antibiotics (based on culture); (2) Source control if needed; (3) Hemodynamic resuscitation; (4) Organ support. Supportive measures for coagulopathy are ADJUNCTIVE, not primary treatment. No specific anti-DIC therapy has proven mortality benefit - treating the cause is essential.

whyChain.answer: Transfusion is SUPPORTIVE, not definitive: (1) Platelets: if <50K AND bleeding or need for procedure; (2) FFP: if PT/PTT prolonged AND bleeding; (3) Cryoprecipitate: if fibrinogen <100-150 mg/dL AND bleeding; (4) Do NOT transfuse based on labs alone - treat BLEEDING patients. Prophylactic transfusion is controversial.

Controversial. Guidelines suggest considering LOW-DOSE heparin (VTE prophylaxis dose) in patients with DIC who have THROMBOSIS-predominant phenotype without active bleeding. NOT recommended in bleeding-predominant DIC. Most septic DIC patients should NOT receive therapeutic heparin. This patient is bleeding - heparin contraindicated.

sources.text: StatPearls: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Wada H, et al. Guidance for diagnosis and treatment of DIC from harmonization of the recommendations from three guidelines. J Thromb Haemost. 2013. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient continues to ooze from IV sites. Fibrinogen is 95 mg/dL.
questions.question: How should you manage the bleeding and low fibrinogen?
questions.hint: Consider transfusion thresholds in active bleeding.

options.text: Only give platelets
Transfuse only when hemoglobin drops
Fibrinogen level doesn't matter
Cryoprecipitate to raise fibrinogen to >100-150 mg/dL (target >150 if severe bleeding); also consider FFP for factor replacement and platelets if <50K; continue treating underlying sepsis

questions.explanation: Transfusion in bleeding DIC: (1) CRYOPRECIPITATE: Contains concentrated fibrinogen (and factor VIII, XIII, vWF). Give if fibrinogen <100-150 mg/dL with bleeding. Target fibrinogen >150 mg/dL in severe bleeding. 10 units raises fibrinogen ~50-70 mg/dL; (2) FFP: If PT/PTT prolonged with bleeding. 15-30 mL/kg; (3) PLATELETS: If <50K with bleeding (or <20K prophylactically in high-risk); (4) Consider fibrinogen concentrate if available. This patient needs cryoprecipitate (fibrinogen 95) + likely FFP + platelets.

whyChain.answer: Generally NOT recommended in DIC - may worsen microvascular thrombosis. Exception: Hyperfibrinolytic DIC (trauma-induced, APL) where bleeding predominates and fibrinolysis is excessive. Not indicated for this septic DIC patient.

sources.text: StatPearls: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Wada H, et al. Guidance for diagnosis and treatment of DIC from harmonization of the recommendations from three guidelines. J Thromb Haemost. 2013. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient with acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) presents with severe bleeding.
questions.question: What is unique about DIC in acute promyelocytic leukemia?

options.text: Only thrombosis occurs in APL
No DIC occurs with APL

APL causes HYPERFIBRINOLYTIC DIC - severe bleeding predominates; APL cells release tissue factor AND plasminogen activators; requires aggressive replacement (cryo, FFP, platelets) + ATRA to treat APL; extremely high bleeding mortality if untreated
APL DIC is identical to sepsis DIC

questions.explanation: APL-associated DIC is HYPERFIBRINOLYTIC: (1) APL cells release tissue factor (activates coagulation) AND plasminogen activators (excessive fibrinolysis); (2) Severe BLEEDING is the predominant manifestation; (3) Very high mortality from hemorrhage (especially intracranial) if untreated; (4) Treatment: ATRA (all-trans retinoic acid) differentiates APL cells and rapidly improves DIC; aggressive blood product replacement; consider antifibrinolytics (TXA) in life-threatening bleeding; (5) Do NOT delay ATRA for cytogenetics confirmation if APL suspected.

whyChain.answer: Obstetric DIC (placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, HELLP, retained dead fetus): Can be severe and acute. Treatment: deliver fetus/placenta (removes trigger), aggressive resuscitation, blood products. Amniotic fluid embolism can cause sudden cardiovascular collapse + DIC - very high mortality.

sources.text: StatPearls: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Integrating all information about this septic patient with DIC.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach for this patient?
questions.hint: Prioritize underlying cause while supporting coagulopathy.

options.text: Start therapeutic heparin immediately
Focus only on transfusion, ignore sepsis treatment
No specific treatment needed for DIC

Priority 1: Treat sepsis aggressively (antibiotics, source control, resuscitation); Priority 2: Supportive transfusion for bleeding (cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100, FFP for coagulopathy, platelets if <50K); Monitor: Serial ISTH DIC scores, coagulation studies q12-24h; Avoid: Therapeutic heparin while bleeding

questions.explanation: Complete management: (1) UNDERLYING CAUSE (Priority): Optimize antibiotics for S. pneumoniae, source control if needed, vasopressors for shock, supportive care; (2) SUPPORTIVE TRANSFUSION: Cryoprecipitate 10 units (target fibrinogen >150), FFP 15-30 mL/kg, Platelets to keep >50K given active bleeding; (3) MONITORING: Serial labs q12-24h (platelets, PT, fibrinogen, D-dimer), trend ISTH DIC score, clinical assessment of bleeding/organ function; (4) AVOID: Therapeutic anticoagulation (bleeding predominant), antifibrinolytics (not hyperfibrinolytic phenotype); (5) PROGNOSIS: DIC should improve as sepsis resolves; persistent/worsening DIC suggests inadequate source control or treatment failure.

whyChain.answer: DIC in sepsis significantly increases mortality (up to 40-80% depending on severity). However, with aggressive treatment of underlying sepsis and supportive care, DIC can resolve. The key is treating the cause - DIC-specific therapies have not shown mortality benefit. This patient's DIC should improve if sepsis is controlled.

sources.text: StatPearls: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Wada H, et al. Guidance for diagnosis and treatment of DIC from harmonization of the recommendations from three guidelines. J Thromb Haemost. 2013. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected disseminated intravascular coagulation?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in disseminated intravascular coagulation. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of disseminated intravascular coagulation. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing disseminated intravascular coagulation, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old presents with petechiae, confusion, renal insufficiency, fever, and platelets of 18,000 with schistocytes on smear. What is the most critical immediate intervention?

options.text: Emergent plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) — do not wait for ADAMTS13 results
options.explanation: This pentad (thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with schistocytes, neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, fever) is classic TTP. Per ASH guidelines, plasma exchange must be initiated IMMEDIATELY — mortality without treatment exceeds 90%. Do NOT wait for ADAMTS13 activity levels; start treatment on clinical suspicion.

options.text: Platelet transfusion to prevent intracranial hemorrhage
options.explanation: Platelet transfusion in TTP is CONTRAINDICATED (relative) as it can fuel thrombotic microangiopathy and worsen organ damage. Platelets are consumed in microvascular thrombi, not bleeding.

options.text: Start heparin for presumed DIC
options.explanation: TTP is distinguished from DIC by NORMAL coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen). DIC shows prolonged PT/PTT and low fibrinogen. Treatment differs fundamentally — TTP requires plasmapheresis, not anticoagulation.

options.text: Obtain ADAMTS13 activity level and treat based on results
options.explanation: ADAMTS13 testing takes 2-3 days. With suspected TTP, mortality approaches 90% without treatment. Plasma exchange must begin on clinical suspicion while awaiting confirmatory testing.
